does: This paper demonstrates adversarial example attacks against vision-language models deployed as trusted authorities for content moderation, fact-checking, and product comparison. The authors show that subtle perturbations to images can cause VLMs like GPT-4V, Claude, Gemini, and Grok to produce confident but incorrect outputs about the perturbed content, while humans perceive the original unperturbed image. Unlike jailbreaks targeting model alignment, these attacks exploit the perceptual vulnerability of the vision encoder. The paper evaluates four attack surfaces: identity manipulation, NSFW content evasion, misinformation amplification, and product recommendation poisoning, demonstrating that adversarial examples transfer reliably from public CLIP models to production VLMs with high success rates across hundreds of targeted attacks.
connects: This work builds on decades of adversarial example research initiated by Szegedy et al. and extended through studies of transferability across models. It specifically addresses the threat model of adversarial perturbations against multimodal systems, building on recent work showing vulnerabilities in vision-language models like CLIP. The paper responds to the growing deployment of VLMs as decision-making systems in high-stakes domains and connects to broader research on AI system robustness, trustworthiness, and alignment. It extends prior work on adversarial robustness by demonstrating that transferability of adversarial examples applies to modern production VLMs, showing that defenses developed against academic models may not protect deployed systems.
enables: This work opens investigation into defensive mechanisms for vision-language models operating as authorities in real-world systems. It motivates research into robust perception at the multimodal level, potentially inspiring certified defenses against imperceptible perturbations and input validation strategies. The findings could drive development of human-in-the-loop verification systems for high-stakes VLM decisions, more rigorous testing protocols before deployment, and adversarial training approaches tailored to multimodal models. The paper points toward fundamental questions about whether VLMs can achieve trustworthy perception guarantees and suggests future work on perceptual alignment between humans and AI systems, as well as new threat models for content moderation and recommendation systems relying on visual understanding.
